lens.included_activities: A correctly classified bank holding-company office only if it independently supplies a recurring or repeat outsourced service to unaffiliated external customers while remaining primarily a non-managing holding office. The available classification evidence suggests that this is an exceptional edge case rather than the ordinary establishment model.
lens.excluded_activities: Entities whose primary activity is holding bank equity or exercising ownership control; banks and other operating financial institutions; offices that administer or manage affiliated establishments; captive shared-service units; shells; non-control financing vehicles; and internal reorganizations.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The default lens requires revenue from repeat services sold to external customers. No defensible recurring external-customer billing model was identified for correctly classified bank holding-company offices; ordinary economics arise from ownership and control of affiliates rather than outsourced-service fees.

a.bridge: BLS measures the occupation mix for the broader Management of Companies and Enterprises sector, which combines holding offices with active managing offices, and O*NET describes accounting and managerial tasks. I translate the prevalence of document, reporting, analysis, and monitoring work into a bounded share of current tasks exposed to AI. The proxy likely overstates exposure for a pure holding office because the broader sector includes labor-intensive managing offices, while it may understate exposure where a small holding-office staff is concentrated in financial review and reporting.
a.rationale: The broader sector contains large accounting, bookkeeping, financial-management, office-supervision, and general-management occupations. O*NET identifies record examination, report preparation, control analysis, performance review, and written communication within those jobs. Those activities contain material language and analytical task exposure, but consequential approvals, fiduciary judgment, governance, and regulated accountability remain human work.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit staffing census or payroll extract for bank holding-company offices, paired with task-level time studies separating affiliate governance, regulatory reporting, and routine document work, would materially change the range.
caveats: The occupation evidence is for sector 55 rather than this six-digit code.
The sector includes managing offices that the Census classification distinguishes from non-managing holding offices.
The range is task exposure, not job displacement, and excludes work already automated.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS measures current and near-term AI use by U.S. businesses, including a Finance and Insurance sector rate, rather than the five-year implemented share of exposed tasks in bank holding-company offices. I use those adoption observations as an anchor, then bound implementation below full task exposure because governance, model risk, data controls, validation, and accountable approval constrain deployment. The Finance and Insurance comparison may overstate applicability because NAICS 551111 is classified in sector 55, while the all-business rate may understate adoption by sophisticated financial groups.
rho.rationale: Current business AI use establishes that implementation is underway, but using AI in any business function is much less demanding than operationally implementing exposed work. Five years permits meaningful workflow integration; bank holding-company governance and regulatory obligations prevent assuming that all technically exposed tasks become implemented substitutions.
rho.change_evidence: A survey of bank holding-company controllers, compliance leaders, and technology executives reporting production AI coverage by workflow, together with model-risk audit findings and measured labor-hour changes, would materially change the range.
caveats: BTOS asks about any AI use and does not measure task coverage or labor substitution.
The cited sector comparison is Finance and Insurance, not NAICS 551111.
The five-year conversion from adoption to implemented exposed work is judgmental.

e.rationale: Eligibility is estimated near zero because the Census classification identifies a bank holding company as a holding office and separately classifies offices that directly manage affiliated companies. Neither ownership control nor captive affiliate administration is a recurring outsourced service to external customers. A small positive range allows for unusual mixed-revenue or classification-edge establishments without treating them as the normal model.
e.change_evidence: A record-level business register with primary NAICS, independent external-customer service revenue, normalized EBITDA, and affiliate status for every bank holding-company office would directly identify the eligible share.
caveats: No observed count of external-service sellers within this code was found.
Mixed-activity legal entities may be classified by establishment-level primary activity in ways that public company descriptions do not reveal.
The supplied firm-count input may be materially misleading for this lens because it counts holding-company establishments rather than verified outsourced-service firms.

s5.rationale: No defensible eligible-firm cohort exists on which to measure qualifying control transfers. Federal Reserve holding-company approvals concern ownership and regulatory control of banking organizations, not necessarily transfers of recurring external-service operations under the frozen lens.
s5.change_evidence: After an eligible cohort is identified, PitchBook, S&P Capital IQ, or Federal Reserve applications matched to establishment-level service revenue and transaction structure could provide a five-year qualifying-transfer numerator and surviving-firm denominator.
caveats: Holding-company formations, bank acquisitions, and internal reorganizations are not interchangeable with transfers of eligible service firms.
A transaction rate for all bank holding companies would answer a different question.

q.rationale: No recurring external-customer contracts, fee schedules, renewal behavior, or competitive repricing evidence was identified for an eligible bank holding-company service population, so retained gross benefit cannot be estimated without inventing a billing model.
q.change_evidence: Contract samples and cohort-level gross-margin bridges from verified eligible establishments, including renewal repricing and customer concessions after automation, would support this quantity.
caveats: Ownership returns and intercompany allocations are not external service revenue.
No fixed-fee, hourly, or cost-plus mix was observable for the frozen lens.

d5.rationale: The current external-service basket and its constant-price quantity are not observable because the classification describes a holding function rather than an outsourced-service market. A five-year demand ratio therefore has no defensible denominator.
d5.change_evidence: Verified service-line revenue and unit-volume histories from eligible establishments, paired with external-customer budgets and forecast volumes, would establish current quantity and a five-year demand path.
caveats: Growth in bank assets, subsidiaries, or regulatory filings would not directly measure demand for the lens's external service basket.
Sector-level employment or establishment growth mixes holding and managing offices.

o.rationale: Without an identified external service basket or eligible operator population, there is no defensible basis for estimating what share of year-five quantity still requires an accountable operator of this kind rather than software, self-service, or another supplier.
o.change_evidence: Workflow-level customer research for verified eligible services, including willingness to self-serve, software substitution, liability allocation, and requirements for a regulated accountable provider, would support this quantity.
caveats: Regulatory accountability inside a holding company does not by itself establish external customer demand for a holding-company operator.
Any estimate would depend on first defining a qualifying service.

narrative.executive_view: The official classification is structurally at odds with the frozen outsourced-service lens. A bank holding-company office principally owns or controls financial institutions; the Census form separately identifies direct management and centralized support. The packet therefore treats eligible external-service firms as rare classification-edge cases and does not fabricate commercial quantities for a market that was not observed.
narrative.ai_impact: Where staff exist, AI can assist financial-statement review, accounting analysis, report drafting, policy research, monitoring, and document preparation. Human accountability remains central for governance, regulatory interpretation, control decisions, exception handling, and approvals, so task exposure is materially larger than near-term autonomous execution.
narrative.value_capture: No external-customer billing structure was found for correctly classified bank holding-company offices. Savings inside a parent group may accrue to owners, but intercompany cost allocation is outside the external recurring-service lens and cannot establish commercial retention.
narrative.firm_availability: The Census activity choices distinguish bank holding companies from managing offices and operating banks. That makes most establishments counted in this code ineligible by construction; the evidence does not identify a transferable population of qualifying outsourced-service firms.
narrative.demand_durability: Bank ownership, governance, and regulatory reporting can persist, but persistence of those internal functions is not evidence of durable external-customer demand for the frozen service basket. Both the current quantity and the year-five operator-required share remain undefined.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The main risk is category error: treating legal ownership vehicles, internal control functions, or bank transactions as independent service businesses. Broader sector occupation and AI-adoption proxies also mix populations and may poorly represent the small staff of a pure holding office.
narrative.principal_driver: Document-heavy financial oversight and reporting work creates plausible AI task exposure wherever a staffed holding office exists.
narrative.principal_weakness: The industry definition supplies no observable recurring external-customer service model, leaving the eligible firm base and all commercial-demand primitives unsupported.

sources.title: 2022 Economic Census Form MN-55111: Management of Companies and Enterprises
sources.supports: The primary-activity choices distinguish bank holding companies, non-managing other holding companies, financial holding companies, and offices engaged in direct management or centralized administrative support.

sources.title: Management of Companies and Enterprises: NAICS 55
sources.supports: Defines the broader sector, explains its mix of holding and managing establishments, and reports common accounting, financial-management, office-supervision, and general-management occupations.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 551000
sources.supports: Provides the detailed occupational distribution for the broader Management of Companies and Enterprises sector used as the occupation-mix proxy.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Accountants and Auditors
sources.supports: Describes accounting work including examining records, preparing statements and reports, analyzing controls, and advising management, which informs the task-exposure proxy.

sources.title: Large Firms With at Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users
sources.supports: Reports recent U.S. business AI adoption by firm size and sector, including the broad Finance and Insurance comparison used to anchor implementation bounds.

sources.title: Bank Holding Company Act of 1956
sources.supports: Documents the ownership, control, approval, activity, capital, and management constraints surrounding bank and financial holding companies, supporting the governance and population caveats.
